topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door clicked shut behind me, the sound echoing in the sterile waiting room. My palms were slick against the worn fabric of my jeans, and I kept shifting my weight, a nervous tic I hadn't shaken since I was ten. I smoothed down my shirt for the tenth time, acutely aware of the way my collar felt too tight. Dr. Elias Thorne, the man who used to relish making my lunchbox contents the subject of cruel jokes, sat across from me, radiating an unnerving calm. He offered a small, practiced smile, and for a split second, I thought I saw a flicker of that old, sharp mischief in his eyes, now softened by something professional. "Take a seat, Sarah," he murmured. The simple request felt monumental. I sank into the chair, my gaze fixed on the pattern of the carpet, trying desperately to appear unremarkable.

[story 2]

I fiddled with the strap of my handbag, the leather cool beneath my fingers. Every time Dr. Thorne leaned forward to make a point, a knot tightened in my stomach, pulling my shoulders up toward my ears. I watched his hands—long, elegant, completely unlike the rough hands that used to shove me into the locker room. It was jarring. He spoke about trauma and coping mechanisms with an effortless grace that made my own anxieties feel clumsy and loud. When he paused, waiting for my response, I swallowed hard, my throat suddenly dry. I wanted to retreat, to disappear into the beige walls, but I remained rooted to the spot, my jaw tight, acutely aware of every tiny movement I made.

[story 3]

"So, tell me about the incident in the cafeteria," Dr. Thorne prompted, his voice low and steady. The memory hit me like a physical blow—the mocking laughter, the way everyone seemed to watch me. My cheeks burned, a hot flush creeping up my neck. I looked down at my hands, noticing the slight tremor. I tried to formulate an answer, but the words caught in my throat, turning to ash. He didn't rush me. He simply waited, his expression unreadable. That silence was worse than any interrogation. I fidgeted with my watch, the metallic click a tiny, frantic sound in the quiet room, wishing I could somehow rewind the tape and erase the memory of being so utterly exposed.
